1.10.a.info:
ipconfig is the foundational Windows command for verifying IP parameters. Run from Command Prompt (CMD) or PowerShell, it returns a summary of every network adapter the OS knows about: Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth PAN, VPN tunnels (TAP/TUN), loopback, and any virtual adapters created by Hyper-V, VMware, or VirtualBox. For each adapter, it prints the IPv4 address, subnet mask, and default gateway.

What you won't see with bare ipconfig: the MAC address, DHCP server, lease times, DNS servers, or WINS info. Those require ipconfig /all. Bare ipconfig is optimized for speed — the answer to 'what's my IP?' in under a second.

Reading the output: Look for the 'Windows IP Configuration' header at the top — that's the dead giveaway this is Windows. Each adapter is listed under its own block like Ethernet adapter Ethernet0: or Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi:. If an adapter is disabled or unplugged, you'll see Media State . . . : Media disconnected and no IP info. A valid active adapter shows three lines: IPv4 Address, Subnet Mask, Default Gateway.

Common exam trap: The subnet mask appears in dotted-decimal form (255.255.255.0) — this is how you distinguish Windows output from Linux output, which uses CIDR (/24). If you see IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . . . . : 192.168.1.50 followed by a dotted-decimal mask, it is Windows ipconfig.

Quick verification workflow: Run ipconfig. Scan for the active adapter. Confirm IP is in the expected subnet, mask matches the network design, and gateway is the correct router/SVI IP. If any are missing or wrong, escalate to ipconfig /all to get the full picture.

nodes: Read IPv4, Mask, Gateway per adapter

hack.memory: Bare ipconfig = 'the three-line answer' — IP, Mask, Gateway. Nothing more. If you need DNS, MAC, or DHCP info, you need the /all flag. Mnemonic: 'IMG' — IP, Mask, Gateway — the photo of your adapter's L3 state.
hack.practice: Open CMD on your Windows PC (or a Packet Tracer PC in Command Prompt mode). Run ipconfig. Identify the active adapter. Write down the three values. Then unplug Ethernet or disable Wi-Fi and re-run — watch 'Media disconnected' appear. Re-enable and re-run. This builds the habit of reading the output in under 3 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 introduces ipconfig. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 documents all variations. The exam often shows ipconfig output and asks 'what OS is this?' or 'what is the host's default gateway?' — dotted-decimal masks and 'Windows IP Configuration' header are the tells.

1.10.a.info:
ipconfig /all is the full-depth Windows IP report. It takes bare ipconfig output and adds every piece of metadata the OS knows about the adapter. This is the go-to command for real troubleshooting because the bare command hides most of the information you actually need.

Extra fields added by /all:

  • Physical Address — the adapter's MAC, in hex with dashes (e.g., 00-1A-2B-3C-4D-5E).
  • DHCP Enabled — Yes or No. Tells you instantly whether the host uses DHCP or has a static IP.
  • DHCP Server — the IP of the DHCP server that issued the current lease. Useful for verifying the client reached the expected server, not a rogue DHCP.
  • Lease Obtained / Lease Expires — timestamps of the current lease. If the lease expired, the client should have renewed — if it didn't, there's a DHCP issue.
  • DNS Servers — primary and secondary DNS IPs. Critical for DNS troubleshooting.
  • Connection-specific DNS Suffix / Primary DNS Suffix — domain name appended to unqualified hostnames.
  • NetBIOS over Tcpip — Enabled / Disabled.

The DHCP vs Static test: The single most useful field is DHCP Enabled. If it says 'Yes,' the host got its config from a DHCP server (and you'll see DHCP Server, Lease Obtained, Lease Expires). If it says 'No,' the IP was manually configured and those DHCP fields will be missing. This one field often answers 'why is this host misconfigured?'

Output size: On a PC with multiple adapters (Ethernet + Wi-Fi + virtual adapters from VMs), ipconfig /all can scroll a long way. Pipe to more (ipconfig /all | more) or findstr (ipconfig /all | findstr /I "DHCP DNS Physical") to filter.

Exam relevance: Extremely high. Exam questions show ipconfig /all output and ask you to identify the DHCP server, the primary DNS, the MAC, or whether the host uses DHCP. Practice parsing this output until each field is instantly recognizable.

hack.memory: /all = 'everything the adapter knows about itself.' Mnemonic for the added fields: 'MacDaddy's Lease Dines on DNS' — MAC, DHCP (server, enabled), Lease (obtained, expires), DNS (servers, suffix). If DHCP Enabled = No, the admin typed the IP by hand.
hack.practice: Run ipconfig /all on your Windows PC. Find the active adapter and identify every single field. Write down: MAC, DHCP Server IP, Lease Obtained timestamp, DNS servers. Then in Packet Tracer, set up a PC with DHCP and a PC with a static IP. Run ipconfig /all on each — compare the 'DHCP Enabled' field (Yes vs No) and note what fields disappear when static.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 walks through ipconfig /all output. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Expect 1-2 exam questions showing this output and asking 'what is the DNS server?' or 'is this host using DHCP?' — instant recognition is the goal.

1.10.a.info:
ipconfig /release tells the Windows DHCP client to give up its current lease. The client sends a DHCPRELEASE unicast message to the DHCP server that issued the lease, the server marks the address as available again, and the client zeros out its IP configuration. The adapter's IPv4 address becomes 0.0.0.0, the subnet mask becomes 0.0.0.0, and network connectivity on that adapter is lost.

When to use it: As the first step in forcing a fresh DHCP lease. Typical scenarios:

  • Host has an APIPA address (169.254.x.x) — DHCP failed, you want to retry.
  • Host is on the wrong subnet after being moved (e.g., laptop moved from office to conference room VLAN).
  • DHCP pool was changed and you want the client to pick up new options (new DNS, new gateway).
  • Troubleshooting DHCP issues from scratch.

Syntax variations: ipconfig /release releases all adapters. ipconfig /release "Ethernet" (or another adapter name) releases just one. Use quotes if the name has spaces. On Windows 10+ PowerShell, you can also use Remove-NetIPAddress for more granular control.

Important: /release is only meaningful for DHCP-configured adapters. If an adapter has a static IP (DHCP Enabled = No), /release has no effect — there's no lease to release. Running it and seeing the IP unchanged is usually a signal that the adapter is statically configured.

Admin privileges: On modern Windows, some adapters (especially on domain-joined machines) require running CMD/PowerShell as Administrator for /release to succeed. Running without elevation may silently fail or return 'The requested operation requires elevation.'

hack.memory: /release = 'drop the lease, go to 0.0.0.0.' Think of it as returning a rental car — you hand back the keys (the DHCP server marks the IP available) and now you have nothing. Always paired with /renew in the next step.
hack.practice: On a DHCP-enabled Windows PC, run ipconfig /release. Verify with ipconfig that the IPv4 Address now reads 0.0.0.0. Try to ping the gateway — fails. Then run ipconfig /renew and watch a new lease arrive. Do this in Packet Tracer too — pick a DHCP-enabled PC, run the release, watch the IP clear. This trains the 'release → verify 0.0.0.0 → renew' muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 47 (DHCP) both cover release/renew. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam scenario: 'A host has a 169.254.x.x address. What command should you run first?' → ipconfig /release followed by /renew. Or the exam may ask 'what does ipconfig /release do?' — it drops the DHCP lease and sets the IP to 0.0.0.0.

1.10.a.info:
ipconfig /renew triggers the Windows DHCP client to request a fresh lease. The exact behavior depends on the current state:

  • If the adapter has no IP (after a /release or on boot), the client runs the full DORA process: Discover (broadcast to find DHCP servers) → Offer (server proposes a lease) → Request (client selects one) → Acknowledge (server confirms).
  • If the adapter still has a valid lease and you run /renew, the client sends a DHCPREQUEST directly to the original DHCP server to extend the existing lease. No full DORA, just the request/ack. This is faster and happens automatically at the lease renewal threshold (T1 = 50% of lease time).

Full DORA — the 4-step exchange:

  1. DHCPDISCOVER — client broadcasts to 255.255.255.255 from 0.0.0.0 looking for any DHCP server.
  2. DHCPOFFER — server responds with a proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease time.
  3. DHCPREQUEST — client broadcasts its selection (in case multiple servers offered, one wins).
  4. DHCPACK — winning server confirms the lease. Client applies the config.

Troubleshooting with /renew: If /renew returns an error like 'An error occurred while renewing interface Ethernet : unable to contact your DHCP server,' the client could not find a DHCP server — likely causes: DHCP server is down, the relay agent (ip helper-address) on the router isn't configured, the VLAN is wrong, or a cable is unplugged. If /renew succeeds and assigns 169.254.x.x, that's APIPA — technically not DHCP, but Windows falls back to it when DHCP fails.

Combined syntax: The standard sequence is ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew in CMD (or two separate commands). This forces a full fresh DORA regardless of current lease state.

hack.memory: /renew = 'grab a fresh lease.' Remember DORA = Discover, Offer, Request, Ack. If the adapter has no IP, you get the full 4-step DORA. If it already has a lease, you just get R/A (request + ack — existing lease extended). Movie mnemonic: 'Dora the Explorer explores for IPs.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up a DHCP server and a client PC. On the client, run ipconfig /release → IP becomes 0.0.0.0. Turn on Simulation mode. Run ipconfig /renew. Step through each packet — you'll see DHCPDISCOVER (broadcast), DHCPOFFER (unicast back), DHCPREQUEST (broadcast), DHCPACK (unicast). Watch the IP populate. This single lab cements DORA forever.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 47 (DHCP) both cover the full DORA exchange. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 and Chapter 7 (DHCP). Exam loves DORA ordering — 'what is the second step of DHCP?' → Offer. Also tests symptom-to-fix: '169.254.x.x address' → ipconfig /release then /renew.

1.10.a.info:
ipconfig /flushdns clears the Windows local DNS resolver cache. Windows caches DNS responses to avoid re-querying DNS servers for names it recently resolved — this speeds up browsing but can cause stale entries if DNS records change.

Why the cache exists: Every time a Windows process resolves a hostname (browser, ping, app), the result is stored in the resolver cache for the TTL specified by the DNS server (typically 300-3600 seconds). Subsequent lookups for the same name return instantly from cache without hitting the DNS server. This reduces DNS traffic and improves perceived performance.

Why you'd flush it:

  • A DNS record was changed (e.g., website moved to a new IP) but the client is still hitting the old IP.
  • Troubleshooting split-DNS issues where internal and external views of a hostname differ and the client cached the wrong one.
  • Clearing negative cache entries — Windows also caches failed lookups ('NXDOMAIN') for a short period. If a domain came online after a failed lookup, flushing lets Windows try fresh.
  • Clearing cache after switching VPN connections or DNS servers.

Related commands:

  • ipconfig /displaydns — show the contents of the resolver cache. Long output; each entry shows the hostname, record type (A, AAAA, CNAME), TTL, and data.
  • ipconfig /registerdns — force re-registration with the DNS server (relevant in Active Directory environments).

Exam relevance: Medium. The exam focuses more on basic IP verification than DNS cache management, but the concept of 'flushing stale DNS' appears in troubleshooting scenarios. Know that /flushdns does NOT change DNS server config — it only clears the local cache.

hack.memory: /flushdns = 'wipe the DNS memory.' Like clearing a browser cache, but for name → IP mappings. Doesn't change DNS servers, doesn't touch DHCP — just erases what Windows 'remembered.' Mnemonic: 'flush = toilet for cached names.'
hack.practice: On Windows, run ipconfig /displaydns — you'll see dozens of cached entries (many from browser activity). Note one. Run ipconfig /flushdns. Run ipconfig /displaydns again — most entries are gone (some system entries from hosts file persist). Open a browser and visit a site, then check /displaydns again — the new entry appears.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 mentions flushdns in passing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam scenario: 'A website's IP changed, but the client still hits the old IP. What command fixes it?' → ipconfig /flushdns. Low-frequency but cheap to memorize.

1.10.a.info:
The command ipconfig does not exist on Linux or macOS. This is a critical exam fact. If you see ipconfig in a command prompt or see output starting with 'Windows IP Configuration,' the OS is Windows — period.

The OS → command mapping:

  • Windows → ipconfig (all flavors: /all, /release, /renew, /flushdns, /displaydns)
  • Linux → ip addr (modern, iproute2 suite) or ifconfig (legacy, net-tools, deprecated)
  • macOS → ifconfig (BSD heritage, still primary) plus networksetup and ipconfig — but wait, macOS DOES have an ipconfig!

macOS ipconfig gotcha: macOS includes a tool called ipconfig, but it is NOT the same tool as Windows ipconfig. The macOS ipconfig is a BSD utility focused on DHCP (e.g., ipconfig getpacket en0 shows the full DHCP lease details for interface en0, ipconfig getifaddr en0 returns just the IP). It does NOT display a comprehensive IP/mask/gateway summary like Windows. For general verification on macOS, use ifconfig or networksetup, not ipconfig.

Why the exam cares: Many CCNA questions present command output with the header and ask you to identify the OS. Trigger words:

  • 'Windows IP Configuration' header → Windows ipconfig
  • 'eth0' / 'ens33' interface names + CIDR masks (/24) → Linux ip addr
  • 'en0' / 'lo0' interface names + hex or BSD-style output → macOS ifconfig

Bottom line for the exam: When someone says 'ipconfig,' assume Windows unless the question explicitly says macOS. When someone says 'ip addr' or 'ss,' that's Linux. When someone says 'ifconfig' alone, it could be either Linux legacy or macOS — context (interface names, mask format) tells you which.

right.label: ipconfig does NOT exist
items: Modern Linux default is ip addr
macOS has a different ipconfig tool

hack.memory: 'ipconfig' = Windows stamp. Header 'Windows IP Configuration' = 100% Windows. Linux = ip addr. macOS = ifconfig (plus its own quirky ipconfig for DHCP only). Mnemonic: 'Windows paid for the ipconfig trademark — Linux and Mac use other names.'
hack.practice: Memorize the OS command triangle by writing it out 5 times: Windows = ipconfig, Linux = ip addr, macOS = ifconfig. Then take 3 screenshots of command output from Jeremy's IT Lab or from your own systems — one per OS. Stare at each until you can identify the OS in 1 second just from the output format.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. The exam definitely tests this — a classic question shows output and asks 'which OS?' or 'what command was used?' The dotted-decimal mask + Windows header is the ironclad Windows tell. If you see CIDR (/24), it's not Windows.

1.10.b.info:
ip addr (shorthand ip a) is the modern Linux command for displaying all network interfaces and their addresses. It is part of the iproute2 package, which replaced the older net-tools package (ifconfig, route, netstat) on virtually all modern Linux distributions: Ubuntu 18.04+, RHEL/CentOS 7+, Fedora 15+, Arch, Debian 9+.

What it shows: Every network interface with its index, name, state (UP/DOWN/UNKNOWN), MTU, MAC address, IPv4 addresses in CIDR notation, IPv6 addresses, and flags. Example output:

2: ens33: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500
    link/ether 00:0c:29:a1:b2:c3 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 192.168.1.50/24 brd 192.168.1.255 scope global ens33
    inet6 fe80::20c:29ff:fea1:b2c3/64 scope link

Reading the output:

  • ens33 — interface name (modern 'predictable' naming — ens = Ethernet, built-in slot 33).
  • UP,LOWER_UP — the kernel considers the interface administratively UP and the physical link is UP (cable plugged, carrier detected).
  • inet 192.168.1.50/24 — the IPv4 address and CIDR mask. CIDR is the Linux tell — Windows uses dotted-decimal (255.255.255.0).
  • link/ether — the MAC address.

Common filters: ip -4 addr (IPv4 only), ip -6 addr (IPv6 only), ip addr show ens33 (just one interface), ip -br addr (brief one-line-per-interface summary, easier to scan).

Why iproute2 replaced net-tools: The old tools were maintained separately, had inconsistent syntax, and didn't handle modern features (multiple IPs per interface, namespaces, VLANs, bridges, VXLAN) well. ip is one unified tool with consistent subcommands: ip addr, ip route, ip link, ip neigh, ip tunnel, ip netns. It's more powerful and the direction Linux is heading.

right.label: Legacy (net-tools)
items: May need install: apt install net-tools

hack.memory: ip addr = 'show me all the addresses.' CIDR notation (/24) = Linux tell. Shorthand ip a saves keystrokes. 'inet' = IPv4, 'inet6' = IPv6, 'link/ether' = MAC. Interface names on modern Linux: ens33, eno1, enp0s3, wlan0, wlp2s0 — NOT eth0 on most new distros (predictable naming).
hack.practice: If you have a Linux VM or WSL: run ip addr. Identify each interface, its state, IP/CIDR, and MAC. Compare to ifconfig (install net-tools if needed: sudo apt install net-tools). Note the format differences — CIDR vs dotted mask, single-block vs multi-line layout. Run ip -br addr to see the compact view. Memorize this output format — the exam shows it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 briefly covers Linux commands. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam question: 'which command shows IP addresses on modern Linux?' → ip addr (NOT ifconfig, which is deprecated). Or the exam shows output with 'inet 10.0.0.5/24' and asks 'what OS?' → Linux.

1.10.b.info:
ip route (shorthand ip r) displays the Linux routing table. This is the Linux equivalent of Windows route print or Cisco's show ip route. It shows every known route, including the default gateway, directly connected networks, and any static or dynamic routes installed.

Typical output:

default via 192.168.1.1 dev ens33 proto dhcp metric 100
192.168.1.0/24 dev ens33 proto kernel scope link src 192.168.1.50 metric 100

Reading each field:

  • default via 192.168.1.1 — the default gateway. 'default' is Linux's shorthand for 0.0.0.0/0. via is followed by the gateway IP.
  • dev ens33 — the outgoing interface used to reach that route.
  • proto dhcp — how the route was installed. Common values: kernel (auto-generated connected route), dhcp (received from DHCP), static (manually configured), ra (IPv6 Router Advertisement), zebra/bird (dynamic routing daemon).
  • metric 100 — preference when multiple routes exist (lower wins, similar to AD+metric combined).
  • src 192.168.1.50 — the source IP this route prefers.

Common operations:

  • ip route — show the full routing table.
  • ip route get 8.8.8.8 — show which route the kernel would use for a specific destination (great for troubleshooting 'which interface will this traffic exit?').
  • sudo ip route add 10.10.10.0/24 via 192.168.1.254 — add a static route.
  • sudo ip route del 10.10.10.0/24 — delete a static route.

How to find the gateway quickly: ip route | grep default or just look for the 'default via' line. This is the Linux equivalent of reading the 'Default Gateway' field in Windows ipconfig.

hack.memory: ip route = Linux routing table. 'default via X' = default gateway X. Mnemonic: 'default via = the way out.' proto kernel = Cisco's C (connected). proto dhcp = learned from DHCP (usually the default route). proto static = admin typed it.
hack.practice: On Linux, run ip route and identify the default gateway. Run ip route get 1.1.1.1 — note which interface and gateway would be used. Add a fake route: sudo ip route add 10.99.99.0/24 via 192.168.1.254, verify with ip route, then sudo ip route del 10.99.99.0/24. This trains the add/verify/delete pattern.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam question: 'which Linux command shows the default gateway?' → ip route (or route -n on legacy). Also: identifying 'default via' as the gateway line is a common exam scenario.

1.10.b.info:
ifconfig was for decades the primary Linux tool for viewing and configuring network interfaces. It is part of the net-tools package, which has been deprecated since approximately 2011 in favor of the ip command from iproute2. Most modern distributions (Ubuntu 18.04+, RHEL/CentOS 8+, Fedora 15+, Debian 9+) no longer install net-tools by default — running ifconfig returns 'command not found' until you install it.

Why it was replaced:

  • Couldn't handle multiple IPs per interface elegantly (required sub-interfaces like eth0:0).
  • Didn't support modern features (namespaces, advanced routing, VLAN stacking, bridging).
  • Separate tools for related tasks (ifconfig, route, arp, netstat) with inconsistent syntax.
  • Development stalled — kernel features moved faster than net-tools could keep up.

What it still does: When installed, ifconfig shows interface info much like ip addr, but with dotted-decimal masks and multiple IPs shown as sub-interfaces. Example:

eth0: flags=4163<UP,BROADCAST,RUNNING,MULTICAST>  mtu 1500
        inet 192.168.1.50  netmask 255.255.255.0  broadcast 192.168.1.255
        ether 00:0c:29:a1:b2:c3

Key difference from ip addr: ifconfig uses dotted-decimal mask format (255.255.255.0), which actually looks more like Windows output. ip addr uses CIDR (/24). The exam may show ifconfig output and you'll need to identify it as Linux (legacy) based on interface names (eth0, lo) and keywords like 'netmask' and 'ether'.

Installing it if missing: sudo apt install net-tools (Debian/Ubuntu) or sudo yum install net-tools (RHEL/CentOS). Some admins still install it out of habit, and many older scripts assume it exists — so you'll find it in the wild despite being deprecated.

Exam note: The CCNA exam may reference ifconfig because older exam materials included it, and macOS still uses it actively. You should know it exists, know it's deprecated on modern Linux, and know that ip addr is the modern replacement.

hack.memory: ifconfig on Linux = 'the old way.' If it says 'command not found,' the distro is modern (iproute2 only). If it works, someone kept net-tools around for muscle memory. Dotted mask + 'netmask' keyword = ifconfig. CIDR + 'inet X/24' = ip addr.
hack.practice: On Linux: try ifconfig. If missing, install net-tools and run it. Compare output side-by-side with ip addr. Note the mask format difference (dotted vs CIDR) and the interface naming difference (eth0 on old systems, ens33/enp0s3 on new). Then uninstall net-tools: sudo apt remove net-tools — you just graduated to modern Linux.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 mentions ifconfig. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam may ask 'what replaced ifconfig on modern Linux?' → ip addr (iproute2). Or 'what output format does ifconfig use for subnet mask?' → dotted decimal.

1.10.b.info:
ss (short for socket statistics) is the modern Linux replacement for the deprecated netstat command. It shows active TCP and UDP connections, listening ports, and socket details directly from kernel socket tables — faster and more accurate than netstat, which had to parse /proc files.

Core use cases:

  • See what's listening: ss -tuln
  • See established connections: ss -tu or ss -tan (all TCP, numeric)
  • See connections for a specific port: ss -tan sport = :80 or ss -tan dport = :443
  • See the process using a socket: sudo ss -tulnp (adds process name/PID)

Flag reference:

  • -t — TCP sockets only
  • -u — UDP sockets only
  • -l — Listening sockets only
  • -n — Numeric (don't resolve hostnames/ports)
  • -p — Show process info (requires sudo for other users' processes)
  • -a — All sockets (listening + established)

Typical output (ss -tuln):

Netid  State   Recv-Q  Send-Q  Local Address:Port   Peer Address:Port
udp    UNCONN  0       0       0.0.0.0:68           0.0.0.0:*
tcp    LISTEN  0       128     0.0.0.0:22           0.0.0.0:*
tcp    LISTEN  0       128     127.0.0.1:631        0.0.0.0:*

Read top to bottom: UDP port 68 (DHCP client) is bound, TCP port 22 (SSH) is listening on all interfaces, TCP port 631 (CUPS printing) is listening on localhost only.

Why not netstat? netstat (from net-tools) is deprecated on modern Linux for the same reasons as ifconfig — slow, inconsistent, missing features. Older exam materials and many online guides still reference netstat; know that ss is the modern equivalent with essentially the same flag conventions.

Exam relevance: Low for pure IP-parameter verification, but ss is useful for the broader 'troubleshoot connectivity' scenarios. Know that ss replaces netstat on modern Linux.

hack.memory: ss = 'socket stats,' 'super speedy.' Common combo: ss -tuln = TCP + UDP + Listening + Numeric. Add -p for process info (needs sudo). Replaces netstat 1:1 with the same flag letters.
hack.practice: On Linux, run ss -tuln. Identify every listening port and map it to a service (22=SSH, 80=HTTP, 443=HTTPS, 53=DNS, 68=DHCP client, etc.). Run sudo ss -tulnp — now you see which process owns each port. Compare to netstat -tuln (if installed). Same output, but ss is faster and the modern choice.
hack.meta: Rarely appears on CCNA directly, but supports broader troubleshooting questions. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 touches on it. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. The key exam fact: ss is the modern replacement for netstat on Linux.

1.10.b.info:
Linux interface names are a subtle but reliable way to identify the OS from command output. Different distros and naming schemes produce different patterns, and the CCNA exam tests the ability to recognize them.

Traditional names (older distros, pre-2015 default):

  • eth0, eth1, eth2 — wired Ethernet interfaces (numbered in discovery order)
  • wlan0, wlan1 — wireless interfaces
  • lo — loopback (127.0.0.1)

Predictable Network Interface Names (systemd default, RHEL 7+, Ubuntu 15.10+, most modern distros):

  • ens33, ens160 — 'ens' = Ethernet, 's' = slot number (common in VMware VMs)
  • eno1, eno2 — 'eno' = Ethernet, 'o' = onboard (motherboard-integrated)
  • enp0s3, enp0s8 — 'enp' = Ethernet, 'p0' = PCI bus 0, 's3' = slot 3 (common in VirtualBox)
  • wlp2s0 — 'wlp' = wireless LAN, 'p2s0' = PCI bus 2 slot 0

Why the change: The old eth0 naming was non-deterministic — on systems with multiple NICs, which one got 'eth0' could change between reboots, breaking configs. Predictable names are tied to hardware location, so they stay consistent.

Other interface types:

  • docker0, br-xxxx — Docker bridge interfaces
  • veth... — virtual Ethernet (container pairs)
  • tun0, tap0 — VPN/tunnel interfaces (OpenVPN, WireGuard often)
  • wg0 — WireGuard interface

OS identification from interface names:

  • ens33, eno1, enp0s3, eth0, wlan0 → Linux
  • en0, en1, lo0, bridge0 → macOS (BSD convention)
  • Ethernet adapter Ethernet, Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi → Windows

Practical tip: If you manage Linux servers, know that ip link lists all interfaces with just their names and states — useful when writing scripts that need to target the primary interface without hardcoding a name.

hack.memory: Interface name = OS fingerprint. 'eth0' or 'ens33' or 'wlan0' = Linux. 'en0' or 'lo0' = macOS. 'Ethernet adapter' or 'Wireless LAN adapter' = Windows. Mnemonic: Linux gets creative (eth, ens, enp, wlan), Mac keeps it short (en0), Windows spells it out in full words.
hack.practice: Pull up command output from three OSes (Windows ipconfig, Linux ip addr, macOS ifconfig). Cover the headers. Just by looking at interface names, identify each OS. Do this 5 times with different outputs until it's instinctive. Worth a question or two on the exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 touches on Linux naming conventions. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam trap: showing Linux output with 'ens33' or similar and asking 'what OS?' — the unfamiliar name is the tell. Memorize the patterns.

1.10.c.info:
macOS retained ifconfig as its standard command-line tool for viewing and configuring network interfaces, even though the Linux world moved to ip addr. This is because macOS is built on Darwin, which inherits from BSD Unix, and ifconfig has always been the BSD tool. Apple has no reason to deprecate it — it works, it's documented, and the Mac admin community is used to it.

What it shows: Running ifconfig with no arguments lists every interface with its state, MTU, MAC, IP addresses, and flags. Example macOS output:

en0: flags=8863<UP,BROADCAST,SMART,RUNNING,SIMPLEX,MULTICAST> mtu 1500
        ether a4:83:e7:12:34:56
        inet 192.168.1.50 netmask 0xffffff00 broadcast 192.168.1.255
        inet6 fe80::1234:5678/64 prefixlen 64
        nd6 options=201<PERFORMNUD,DAD>
        media: autoselect
        status: active

macOS-specific quirks in the output:

  • netmask 0xffffff00 — macOS shows masks in hexadecimal by default. 0xffffff00 = 255.255.255.0 = /24. (Linux ifconfig uses decimal 'netmask 255.255.255.0'.)
  • status: active — BSD-style status indicator. 'inactive' means the interface is down or not configured.
  • media: autoselect — link type/speed negotiated automatically.

Targeting one interface: ifconfig en0 shows just the primary interface. ifconfig -a shows all (including inactive). ifconfig lo0 shows loopback.

Configuration: You can change interfaces with sudo ifconfig en0 inet 10.1.1.50 netmask 255.255.255.0, but this change doesn't survive reboots — macOS uses SystemConfiguration / networksetup for persistent config. Temporary ifconfig changes are fine for troubleshooting.

Exam fingerprints for macOS: hex netmask (0xffffff00), interface names like en0/en1/lo0/bridge0, 'ether' keyword for MAC, 'status: active' line. If you see these, it's macOS — not Linux.

hack.memory: macOS kept ifconfig because BSD. Hex netmask (0xffffff00) = macOS tell. 'en0' short name = macOS. Linux ifconfig uses decimal mask, Linux uses 'eth0'. Mnemonic: 'Mac = hex, Linux = decimal' for mask format.
hack.practice: On your Mac, open Terminal and run: ifconfig en0. Note the hex netmask — convert 0xffffff00 by hand: ff=255, ff=255, ff=255, 00=0 → 255.255.255.0 → /24. Do this 3 times with different masks. Then run ifconfig -a and note loopback (lo0), bridge (bridge0 if you have Docker), and any active/inactive interfaces.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 touches on macOS. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam relevance is low — 1 question at most — but know the fingerprints. Hex mask + en0 + 'ether' = macOS.

1.10.c.info:
macOS interface naming follows BSD conventions, which are distinct from Linux and Windows. The names are short, numeric-suffixed, and tied to physical hardware order.

Standard interface names:

  • en0 — primary Ethernet or Wi-Fi adapter. On most Macs today (especially laptops), en0 is actually the Wi-Fi card because there's no built-in Ethernet port.
  • en1, en2, en3... — secondary adapters. USB-C Ethernet dongles, Thunderbolt Ethernet, or a second Wi-Fi card all get incrementing 'en' numbers.
  • lo0 — loopback interface (equivalent to Linux's lo, carrying 127.0.0.1/8).
  • utun0, utun1, ... — tunnel interfaces for VPN connections (native macOS VPN, Cisco AnyConnect, etc.).
  • awdl0 — Apple Wireless Direct Link, used by AirDrop and Continuity features.
  • llw0 — Low-latency WLAN interface (Apple-specific).
  • bridge0 — bridge interface, often created by virtualization tools (Parallels, VMware Fusion) or Internet Sharing.
  • gif0, stf0 — tunnel interfaces for IPv6 transition (generic IPv4→IPv6 gif, 6to4 stf).
  • ap1 — hotspot access point interface when Mac shares its internet.

Figuring out which is your active adapter:

  • networksetup -listallhardwareports — cleanest mapping of friendly names ('Wi-Fi') to device names ('en0').
  • route get default — shows which interface carries your default route (tells you which enN is actually active).
  • System Settings → Network — GUI view with friendly names.

Why this matters: When scripting or troubleshooting on macOS, you need to reference the right enN. Assuming en0 is Ethernet (like on old desktop Macs) breaks on modern laptops where en0 is Wi-Fi. Always verify with networksetup -listallhardwareports.

Exam relevance: Low. One possible question: 'what is the primary interface name on macOS?' → en0 (or sometimes en1 depending on hardware). Or 'what's the macOS loopback?' → lo0.

hack.memory: macOS: en0 = primary (Wi-Fi on laptops, Ethernet on desktops), lo0 = loopback. 'utun' = VPN tunnel. 'bridge0' = Parallels/VMware/Internet Sharing. Mnemonic: 'en' for 'Ethernet Network' (even though it's often Wi-Fi now — legacy naming).
hack.practice: On your Mac: ifconfig (scan the list). Then networksetup -listallhardwareports and map friendly names to device names. Note which enN is your active internet interface: route get default shows it. Do this once and you'll have the map memorized.
hack.meta: Very low exam priority — maybe a flashcard. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 briefly covers macOS. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Focus on knowing en0 = primary adapter, lo0 = loopback. That's enough for the exam.

1.10.c.info:
networksetup is an Apple-specific, higher-level network configuration CLI on macOS. It sits above ifconfig and manages the macOS System Preferences / System Settings network configuration — meaning changes made with networksetup persist across reboots, unlike raw ifconfig changes.

Common read-only queries:

  • networksetup -listallhardwareports — list all network services with friendly names ('Wi-Fi', 'USB 10/100/1000 LAN') and device names ('en0', 'en5'). The cleanest way to map names.
  • networksetup -listallnetworkservices — list just the service names, optionally with enabled/disabled state.
  • networksetup -getinfo "Wi-Fi" — show IP, subnet mask, router (gateway), and link-local address for a specific service. Clean, human-readable output.
  • networksetup -getdnsservers "Wi-Fi" — just the DNS servers configured for that service.
  • networksetup -getcomputername — Mac's hostname / computer name.

Example of getinfo output:

DHCP Configuration
IP address: 192.168.1.50
Subnet mask: 255.255.255.0
Router: 192.168.1.1
Client ID:
IPv6: Automatic
IPv6 IP address: none
IPv6 Router: none
Wi-Fi ID: a4:83:e7:12:34:56

Read it top-down: DHCP Configuration header tells you it's DHCP-assigned (would say 'Manual Configuration' otherwise). IP, mask, router — the three essential fields. Way cleaner than parsing ifconfig output.

Common write operations (need sudo):

  • sudo networksetup -setdnsservers "Wi-Fi" 1.1.1.1 8.8.8.8 — set DNS servers persistently.
  • sudo networksetup -setmanual "Wi-Fi" 192.168.1.100 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.1 — switch to static IP.
  • sudo networksetup -setdhcp "Wi-Fi" — switch back to DHCP.

Why use it over ifconfig: Changes persist through reboots. Output is cleaner and matches what you'd see in System Settings GUI. Scripts are more readable. For quick checks, networksetup -getinfo is one command and gives you exactly the info ipconfig gives on Windows.

Exam relevance: Low. The exam won't drill networksetup syntax, but you should know it exists as the macOS-specific alternative to ifconfig.

hack.memory: networksetup = 'the macOS-friendly network tool.' Key command: networksetup -getinfo "Wi-Fi" = the macOS version of ipconfig. Clean output with IP, mask, router, DNS. Mnemonic: 'ifconfig shows the engine, networksetup shows the dashboard.'
hack.practice: On your Mac: networksetup -listallhardwareports, networksetup -getinfo "Wi-Fi", networksetup -getdnsservers "Wi-Fi". Compare the clean output to ifconfig en0 — same info, but networksetup is reader-friendly. You'll use this tool a lot as a Mac admin.
hack.meta: Low exam weight. Know it exists, know -getinfo is the go-to. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 mentions macOS in passing.

1.10.c.info:
The OS Command Triangle is the single most exam-relevant concept in topic 1.10: every operating system has its own primary command for IP verification, and the exam regularly shows output from one and asks you to identify the OS or recall the equivalent command on another OS.

The triangle:

  • Windows → ipconfig (all variations: /all, /release, /renew, /flushdns)
  • Linux (modern) → ip addr (or shorthand ip a) from iproute2
  • Linux (legacy) / macOS → ifconfig (from net-tools on Linux, BSD on Mac)

Secondary commands (also triangle-mapped):

  • Routing table: Windows = route print or netstat -rn; Linux = ip route; macOS = netstat -rn or route get default
  • Traceroute: Windows = tracert; Linux = traceroute; macOS = traceroute
  • ARP cache: Windows = arp -a; Linux = ip neigh (modern) or arp -a; macOS = arp -a
  • DNS lookup: Windows = nslookup; Linux = dig or nslookup; macOS = dig or nslookup
  • Socket stats: Windows = netstat; Linux = ss (modern); macOS = netstat

Fingerprints for OS identification from output:

  • Windows: 'Windows IP Configuration' header, dotted-decimal masks (255.255.255.0), 'Physical Address' label for MAC, 'Default Gateway' label, 'Ethernet adapter X' adapter names.
  • Linux: CIDR masks (/24) in ip addr; interface names like ens33, eno1, enp0s3, eth0, wlan0; 'link/ether' for MAC; 'inet' and 'inet6' for IPv4/IPv6.
  • macOS: Hex netmask (0xffffff00), interface names like en0, lo0, bridge0, 'ether' for MAC, 'status: active' lines, BSD-style output format.

Exam strategy: When the exam shows command output, the FIRST thing to look for is: (1) header banner, (2) mask format, (3) interface names. These three tell you the OS in seconds. Then you can answer 'which command produced this?' or 'what's the equivalent on Linux?'

hack.memory: The triangle: Windows-ipconfig, Linux-ipaddr, macOS-ifconfig. Mnemonic: 'Windows IP, Linux IP, Mac IF' — the first two start with IP, Mac is the odd one. Output fingerprint: dotted mask = Windows, CIDR = Linux, hex = macOS. Memorize this triangle — it's worth 2-3 exam questions.
hack.practice: Make a 3-column flashcard: Windows | Linux | macOS. Fill in rows for: show IPs, show routes, traceroute, DNS lookup, ARP cache, socket stats. Drill it daily until you can recite each row from memory. This one card covers most of 1.10's exam content.
hack.meta: Guaranteed on the exam — at least one question from this triangle. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 is the best video for the cross-OS comparison. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Every practice exam (Boson, Pearson) hits this.

1.10.c.info:
tracert vs traceroute is one of the classic CCNA OS-difference traps. The underlying concept is identical — trace the path packets take across a network, hop by hop — but the command name, default probe type, and output format differ by OS.

Windows: tracert

  • Uses ICMP Echo Request packets (the same thing ping uses) with incrementing TTL values starting at 1.
  • Output: hop number, 3 RTT measurements (in ms), then the hop's IP or hostname.
  • Max hops default: 30 (change with -h N).
  • Name resolution: on by default (try -d to skip, which is faster).
  • Example: tracert google.com or tracert -d 8.8.8.8.

Linux: traceroute

  • Uses UDP packets to high-numbered ports (33434 by default, incrementing) with incrementing TTL. (Or -I for ICMP like Windows, -T for TCP SYN.)
  • Output format similar to Windows, 3 probes per hop, RTTs and IP/hostname.
  • Linux version is typically not installed by default on minimal Linux images — install with sudo apt install traceroute.
  • Example: traceroute google.com.

macOS: traceroute

  • Same name as Linux: traceroute. Pre-installed on macOS.
  • Default probe: UDP, same as Linux. -I for ICMP.
  • Example: traceroute apple.com.

How TTL-based tracing works: The tool sends packet 1 with TTL=1. The first hop decrements TTL to 0 and returns an ICMP Time Exceeded. The source records the hop's IP. Then packet 2 with TTL=2 — reaches hop 2, which returns Time Exceeded. And so on, until the packet reaches the destination and gets a destination reply (ICMP Echo Reply for tracert; UDP 'port unreachable' for traceroute). This clever use of TTL is why the command works on any IP network.

Common exam questions:

  • 'Which command traces the path on Windows?' → tracert
  • 'Which protocol does Windows tracert use?' → ICMP
  • 'Which protocol does Linux traceroute use by default?' → UDP
  • 'What does * * * in traceroute output mean?' → Timeouts — the hop isn't responding to probes (ICMP/UDP might be filtered by a firewall or ACL).

hack.memory: Windows = tracert (truncated, ICMP). Linux/Mac = traceroute (full word, UDP). Mnemonic: 'Windows shortens, Unix spells.' Protocol: Windows ICMP, Unix UDP by default. The * * * = filtered/blocked hop.
hack.practice: Run tracert 8.8.8.8 on Windows. Run traceroute 8.8.8.8 on Mac/Linux (or a Packet Tracer Linux PC). Compare outputs. Note that some hops show * — these are hops filtering your probes. Now try tracert -d (Windows, no DNS resolution) — much faster. And traceroute -I on Mac/Linux — uses ICMP, may get different results than UDP.
hack.meta: Very high exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Memorize: Windows tracert (ICMP), Linux/Mac traceroute (UDP default). Also know TTL-based hop discovery — the exam may ask 'how does traceroute identify each hop?' → TTL decrementing and ICMP Time Exceeded.

1.10.d.info:
Every IP host, regardless of operating system, needs four parameters correctly configured for full network connectivity: IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server(s). Drop or break any one of these, and you get a specific, predictable failure mode. Memorizing the parameter-to-symptom mapping is the highest-yield troubleshooting skill on the CCNA.

The four parameters and what they enable:

  • IP address — the host's unique L3 identity on the network. Required to send or receive any IP packet. Without it, no connectivity at all.
  • Subnet mask — defines the boundary between 'local' (same subnet, reach via ARP) and 'remote' (different subnet, send to default gateway). Without it or with the wrong value, the host makes wrong forwarding decisions.
  • Default gateway — the IP of the router or L3 switch that provides access to remote networks. Without it, the host can only reach its own subnet.
  • DNS server(s) — resolves hostnames to IPs. Without it, the host can only reach destinations by IP; hostname-based connectivity (including every web browser) fails.

The dependency order: These four build on each other. IP + mask enables local communication. Add gateway → enables remote communication. Add DNS → enables hostname communication. You can verify each layer independently with ping (local + remote by IP) and nslookup/dig (DNS).

How hosts get these parameters:

  • DHCP — the client receives all four in the DHCPOFFER/DHCPACK from the DHCP server. This is the default on virtually every client device.
  • Static — an admin manually types them into the OS network settings. Typical for servers, infrastructure devices, and anything that must have a predictable IP.
  • Mix — a host can have a static IP with DHCP-assigned DNS (using DHCP option 6 only).

Verification commands by OS:

  • Windows: ipconfig /all shows all four.
  • Linux: ip addr (IP + mask) + ip route (gateway) + cat /etc/resolv.conf (DNS).
  • macOS: networksetup -getinfo "Wi-Fi" shows all four cleanly.

The troubleshooting framework: Always verify the four parameters FIRST before investigating anything else. 'ping doesn't work' with no IP check is a waste of time — fix the config before chasing ghosts.

items: Subnet mask — local vs remote boundary
Default gateway — path to remote networks

hack.memory: 'A Man Gets Directions' = Address, Mask, Gateway, DNS. Or 'IMGD' — IP, Mask, Gateway, DNS. Four vital signs of a host. Missing any = predictable failure. Memorize the mapping: no address = nothing works. No mask = boundary confusion. No gateway = local-only. No DNS = names fail, IPs still work.
hack.practice: Pick a host and run ipconfig /all (Windows) or networksetup -getinfo "Wi-Fi" (Mac). Write down all 4 values on paper without looking. Then break each one in Packet Tracer or on a VM and watch the resulting symptom. Four break-and-fix cycles = one mastered exam topic.
hack.meta: Heavily tested. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 47 (DHCP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 and Chapter 7. The exam loves scenarios that break exactly one parameter and ask which one. Master the four-to-symptom mapping and you'll ace these questions.

1.10.d.info:
APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing) is what Windows does when DHCP fails. If a Windows host sends DHCPDISCOVER and receives no DHCPOFFER within the configured timeout (about 6 seconds default), the OS gives up on DHCP and self-assigns an IP from the 169.254.0.0/16 range — specifically 169.254.1.0 through 169.254.254.255. The mask is always 255.255.0.0 (/16). No gateway is set. No DNS is set.

Why 169.254.0.0/16? This range is reserved by IANA (RFC 3927) for link-local addressing — addresses that are valid ONLY on the local network segment, never routed anywhere. Every OS that implements zero-configuration networking uses this range: Windows calls it APIPA, Linux uses Zeroconf/Avahi, macOS does it too.

The exam tell: If you see a host with a 169.254.x.x address, you instantly know:

  • The host tried DHCP.
  • The host received no response.
  • The host self-assigned because Windows/Linux/macOS wanted to keep the interface 'usable' for local-only traffic.

Why APIPA equals 'DHCP broken': With APIPA, the host can ONLY talk to other APIPA hosts on the same physical segment. No gateway means no internet, no inter-subnet traffic. No DNS means no hostname resolution. Effectively, the host is isolated from every useful network service.

Root causes of DHCP failure that lead to APIPA:

  • DHCP server is down — service crashed, VM powered off, switch port to server is down.
  • DHCP server has exhausted its pool — all available IPs are leased.
  • Client is in the wrong VLAN — DHCP server is not reachable from this VLAN.
  • Missing ip helper-address on the router — DHCP broadcasts don't cross router interfaces, so the router needs to relay them. Without this, clients on remote subnets can't reach the central DHCP server.
  • Cable issue or switch port shut down.
  • DHCP snooping blocking the response (misconfigured security).

The fix sequence:

  1. Run ipconfig /release then ipconfig /renew. If a new valid IP arrives, the original DHCP failure was transient.
  2. If still APIPA, check physical connectivity (cable, link light, switch port).
  3. Check VLAN assignment.
  4. Check DHCP server health.
  5. Check ip helper-address on the router if the client is on a different subnet from the DHCP server.

hack.memory: 169.254.x.x = 'DHCP is broken.' APIPA = 'Automatic Private IP Addressing' = the OS giving up. Link-local only — no gateway, no DNS. Fix order: release/renew → cable → VLAN → DHCP server → ip helper-address on router. Mnemonic: '169 = DHCP DEAD.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up a DHCP server and a client. Get a good lease, verify with ipconfig. Now shut the DHCP server (power off the VM/server device). On the client, ipconfig /release then /renew — watch the IP fall to 169.254.x.x. Troubleshoot: turn DHCP server back on, /renew again — good IP returns. Repeat with the VLAN broken — same APIPA symptom, different root cause.
hack.meta: Extremely high exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 47. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 7. Classic exam question: 'A Windows host has IP 169.254.10.5. What is the cause?' → DHCP failed. The range AND the subnet (255.255.0.0) are both testable facts.

1.10.d.info:
A wrong subnet mask is one of the trickiest misconfigurations because connectivity fails in partial, inconsistent ways — some hosts work, others don't, and users call it an 'intermittent' problem. The root cause is that the mask defines where the host thinks the local/remote boundary is, so a wrong mask breaks the local-vs-remote decision.

How the mask drives forwarding decisions: When a host wants to send to destination X, it performs ANDing of its own IP + mask and X + mask. If the results match, X is 'local' — the host ARPs for X's MAC and sends directly. If they don't match, X is 'remote' — the host sends the frame to its default gateway's MAC instead.

Failure mode 1 — Mask too long (e.g., /25 when network is really /24):

  • Host thinks its subnet is smaller than reality.
  • Real neighbors on the 'other half' look remote.
  • Host tries to send to the gateway for those neighbors — gateway routes the frame back, but return path often fails because the other host may be in the same real subnet with its own mask issues.
  • Symptom: half the hosts on the subnet are reachable, half are not.

Failure mode 2 — Mask too short (e.g., /16 when network is really /24):

  • Host thinks its subnet is larger than reality.
  • Hosts on actually-remote subnets look local.
  • Host ARPs for MACs that belong on other subnets — ARP gets no reply (the target isn't on this wire) OR it reaches the wrong host with the same last-octet.
  • Symptom: can't reach remote subnets correctly, may have strange collisions with actual-same-IP hosts in other subnets.

Example scenario: Network is 192.168.1.0/24 (mask 255.255.255.0). Host A at 192.168.1.10 is correctly configured. Host B at 192.168.1.200 has mask 255.255.255.128 (/25) by mistake. When A pings B (.10 → .200, both in 192.168.1.0/24), A thinks it's local, ARPs for .200's MAC, gets a reply, and ping succeeds going OUT. But when B pings A, B's mask says .200 and .10 are in different /25 subnets — B sends the return traffic to its default gateway, which may not route it back correctly. Result: one-way connectivity issues, intermittent failures depending on who initiates.

How to diagnose:

  1. Check mask on ipconfig / ip addr.
  2. Compare to the expected network design (ask the admin, check documentation, check other working hosts on the same VLAN).
  3. Common mistake patterns: /25 instead of /24, /23 instead of /24, /16 instead of /24, or swapping mask octets (255.255.0.255 is invalid).

hack.memory: Wrong mask = 'boundary confusion.' Half-reachable = classic mask-too-long symptom. Remote subnets unreachable = mask-too-short. Mnemonic: 'Mask long = short range, mask short = long range' (inverse relationship between mask length and subnet size).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: 4 PCs on one switch, all supposed to be in 192.168.1.0/24. Give 2 PCs correct mask (/24), give 2 PCs wrong mask (/25). Ping between all combos and track which ones work vs fail. Map the failure pattern. Then fix the masks and re-test. This cements the 'boundary confusion' intuition.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and the subnetting videos. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 11-12 (subnetting) and Chapter 4 (troubleshooting). Exam scenario: 'Host A can ping Host B but not Host C on the same switch. All three have IPs in 192.168.1.0/24. What's the likely cause?' → Wrong subnet mask on one of them.

1.10.d.info:
A missing or wrong default gateway is one of the cleanest symptom-to-cause mappings on the exam: local pings work, remote pings fail. The host can talk to anything on its own subnet but cannot reach any remote network. This is because the gateway is the host's only exit from its own subnet — without it, the host has no idea where to send remote-destined packets.

How it manifests:

  • ping [other host on same subnet] → succeeds. The host ARPs for the neighbor's MAC and sends directly. No gateway needed.
  • ping [default gateway IP] → might succeed or fail depending on whether the gateway is reachable (it is on the same subnet). Usually succeeds IF the IP is correct. If gateway IP is wrong, this fails too.
  • ping [remote IP, e.g., 8.8.8.8] → fails. Host doesn't know where to send this. Windows may say 'Destination host unreachable' immediately (no gateway configured). Linux shows 'Network is unreachable.'
  • ping google.com → fails either at DNS (if DNS needs remote access) or later on the actual ping.

Two flavors of gateway problems:

  1. Gateway is missing entirely. DHCP didn't supply it, or admin forgot to set it in static config. The host has IP + mask but no gateway. Symptom: instant 'destination host unreachable' for any remote ping.
  2. Gateway is configured but wrong. Gateway IP is typed incorrectly or points to an IP that doesn't exist / no longer exists. Host tries to ARP for the wrong IP — ARP fails — remote pings time out. Slightly different symptom: timeouts instead of instant unreachable.

How to diagnose:

  1. ipconfig / ip route / networksetup -getinfo — check if gateway is configured.
  2. If configured, ping [gateway IP] — confirm it responds.
  3. If gateway responds, check the router config: is the gateway IP actually assigned to an interface? Is that interface up/up? Is the router's routing table pointing somewhere useful?

Real-world trap: If the router interface IP is renumbered (e.g., gateway changed from .1 to .254), but DHCP options aren't updated, clients continue to get the old gateway at lease renewal and everyone goes offline. Always update DHCP pool options when changing gateway IPs.

Exam phrasing: 'A user can access devices on their own subnet but cannot reach external networks.' → Missing/wrong default gateway. Nearly guaranteed question type.

right.label: Fails (remote)
items: Host doesn't know where to send

hack.memory: Missing gateway = 'local-only.' Ping neighbor works. Ping anything remote fails instantly. Mnemonic: 'No gateway, no internet.' Quick check: ping [gateway IP] — if you can't even reach the gateway, the IP is wrong or the gateway is down.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up a PC with correct IP/mask and a valid gateway. Verify ping works to both local and remote hosts. Now go to PC settings and DELETE the gateway. Re-test: local ping works, remote ping fails instantly. Then set a WRONG gateway (e.g., .254 when the real one is .1). Retry remote ping — now it times out instead of fail-fast. Two different error modes = two different exam scenarios.
hack.meta: Very high exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 11 (Static Routing). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4 and Chapter 15. Exam scenario: 'A user can ping their printer (same subnet) but cannot reach the internet. What's wrong?' → Default gateway missing or wrong.

1.10.d.info:
The wrong/missing DNS symptom is the most common real-world complaint from users: 'the internet is down.' But ping 8.8.8.8 works fine, while ping google.com fails. This is the textbook DNS failure signature — IP-based connectivity works, hostname-based connectivity doesn't.

The symptom in detail:

  • ping 8.8.8.8 (or any other known IP) → works. Routing and gateway are fine. Packets reach the destination.
  • ping google.com → fails with 'Ping request could not find host google.com. Please check the name and try again.' (Windows) or 'Temporary failure in name resolution' (Linux).
  • Browsers show 'This site can't be reached' / 'DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN' / 'server not found' errors for every website.
  • nslookup google.com or dig google.com → returns 'server can't find' / timeout / SERVFAIL.

Root causes:

  • No DNS server configured. DHCP didn't supply one, or static config left it blank.
  • Wrong DNS server IP. Typo, or server was decommissioned and the admin forgot to update DHCP options.
  • DNS server is down / unreachable. Firewall blocking port 53, DNS service crashed, server offline.
  • DNS server reachable but not answering for the domain. Misconfigured zone, or the DNS server is an internal-only server that doesn't resolve external domains (and there's no forwarder).
  • Stale DNS cache on the client (flushing with ipconfig /flushdns fixes this).

How to diagnose:

  1. Confirm the symptom: ping 8.8.8.8 works, ping google.com doesn't → DNS is the culprit.
  2. Check the configured DNS server: ipconfig /all (Windows), cat /etc/resolv.conf (Linux), scutil --dns (macOS).
  3. ping [DNS server IP] — is the DNS server reachable at all?
  4. nslookup google.com [DNS server IP] — does this specific server respond?
  5. Try a known-good public DNS: nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8. If this works, your configured DNS is the problem.
  6. Flush local cache: ipconfig /flushdns (Windows).

The classic workaround: Change the host's DNS to 8.8.8.8 (Google) or 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare). If the problem disappears, the original DNS server was broken. This is a 30-second fix and a 90% success rate for real-world 'internet is down' calls.

Exam phrasing: 'A user can ping by IP address but not by hostname. What is the problem?' → DNS. This is practically free points.

hack.memory: 'Internet is down' = DNS (90% of the time). Ping IP works + ping name fails = DNS broken. Quick fix: set DNS to 1.1.1.1 or 8.8.8.8. Mnemonic: 'Names fail, IPs prevail = DNS is the wail.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: PC with IP, mask, gateway, and DNS all configured correctly. Add a web server with a hostname. Verify ping [hostname] works. Now change the DNS field on the PC to 192.168.99.99 (non-existent). Re-ping by IP → works. Re-ping by hostname → fails. Change DNS to the actual DNS server → works again. Memorize this exact failure signature.
hack.meta: Highest-frequency real-world troubleshooting call AND exam question. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 48 (DNS). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 (Fundamentals of LANs) and Chapter 18 (DNS). Also flush-related: 'ipconfig /flushdns' clears stale cache. Know all DNS verification commands: nslookup, dig.

1.10.d.info:
IP addressing mismatch is when a host gets an IP in the wrong subnet entirely — not an APIPA address, not a wrong mask, just a valid IP that doesn't belong on this network. The host can exchange frames on the physical wire but can't participate in the L3 subnet it's supposed to be in.

How it happens:

  • Wrong VLAN assignment. The switch port is in VLAN 10 (subnet 10.0.10.0/24) but should be in VLAN 20 (subnet 10.0.20.0/24). DHCP on VLAN 10 hands out 10.0.10.x addresses — but the user thought they were on VLAN 20. Result: host has 10.0.10.50 when it should have 10.0.20.x. Everything 'local' connects fine (to other VLAN 10 hosts) but nothing the user actually wants is reachable.
  • Wrong DHCP scope. DHCP server has multiple scopes, and somehow the host was served from the wrong pool (bad scope selection, misconfigured relay, overlapping scopes).
  • Static IP typo. Admin typed 10.0.20.50 meant to type 10.0.2.50. One character off, but the host is now on a completely different subnet.
  • Laptop moved between buildings/sites without reconnecting to fresh DHCP. Old lease from office A is still active, and now the host is plugged into office B's different subnet but trying to use the old IP.

Symptoms:

  • Host has a valid-looking IP (not APIPA, not 0.0.0.0).
  • Can't reach the expected default gateway — because that gateway is on a different subnet.
  • Can't reach any of the intended servers/services.
  • May be able to reach SOME hosts — the ones that happen to be on the 'wrong' subnet with this host.
  • ipconfig / ip addr shows an IP — looks fine at first glance — but the IP doesn't match what the user/device was supposed to get.

Diagnosis:

  1. Check the IP on the host.
  2. Ask: is this IP in the subnet I EXPECTED for this device? Know the correct subnet for each VLAN / site / building.
  3. If the IP is in the wrong subnet, check: (a) the switch port's VLAN assignment, (b) the DHCP server's scopes and reservations, (c) any static config on the host itself.
  4. Fix: correct the VLAN, update DHCP scope, clear the client's lease (ipconfig /release; ipconfig /renew) so it pulls a fresh IP from the correct pool.

Exam relevance: Medium. Less common than APIPA or gateway issues, but still shows up in 'Which of these hosts is misconfigured?' questions where you compare IPs to expected subnets.

hack.memory: Wrong subnet = 'valid IP, wrong neighborhood.' Usually a VLAN assignment or DHCP scope error. Host has an IP but can't reach its own gateway. Mnemonic: 'Right OS, wrong apartment' — the IP fits an IP format but the address is in the wrong building.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: two VLANs (10 and 20) on a switch, each with its own DHCP scope. Assign a PC port to VLAN 10. Boot the PC — it gets 10.0.10.x. Now move the port to VLAN 20 (SW config) but keep the PC running without renewing. PC still has 10.0.10.x lease — but it's now in VLAN 20 physically. Ping the VLAN 20 gateway → fails. Run ipconfig /release and /renew → now gets 10.0.20.x → ping works. This shows exactly how VLAN moves create subnet mismatches.
hack.meta: Medium frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 (VLANs) and Day 47 (DHCP) both cover this scenario. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8 (VLANs) and 7 (DHCP). Exam scenario: 'Host IP is 10.0.10.50 but should be in VLAN 20 (10.0.20.0/24). What went wrong?' → VLAN or DHCP misconfiguration.

1.10.e.info:
ping is the universal Layer 3 connectivity test. It sends ICMP Echo Request packets to a target IP and listens for ICMP Echo Reply packets. If replies come back, the IP path between source and destination is operational in both directions. If no replies, something is broken at L3, L2, L1, or a firewall is dropping ICMP.

What ping tests:

  • L3 reachability — can IP packets travel source → destination AND destination → source?
  • Round-trip time (RTT) — how long the round trip takes, in milliseconds. Indicates latency and congestion.
  • Packet loss — if you send 4 and get 3 back, 25% loss. Indicates intermittent problems.

Behavior by OS:

  • Windows: sends 4 pings then stops by default. Use ping -t for continuous, ping -n 100 for a specific count, ping -a to resolve the IP back to a hostname.
  • Linux/macOS: sends continuous pings until you press Ctrl+C. Use ping -c 4 to match Windows default. -i [seconds] for interval, -s [size] for payload size.
  • Cisco IOS: sends 5 pings by default. Extended ping (ping with no args in privileged mode) gives you repeat count, size, source interface, DF bit options.

Reading Cisco ping output:

  • ! = success (Echo Reply received).
  • . = timeout (no reply within 2 seconds default).
  • U = destination unreachable (ICMP Destination Unreachable received).
  • Q = source quench (rare, deprecated — indicates congestion).
  • Example: !!!!! = 5/5 success. .!!!! = first timed out (likely ARP resolution), then 4/4 success.

The ping ladder (troubleshooting sequence):

  1. ping 127.0.0.1 — tests the local TCP/IP stack. If this fails, the OS networking is broken (very rare).
  2. ping [own IP] — tests the NIC and driver. If this fails, NIC hardware or driver issue.
  3. ping [default gateway] — tests local network connectivity.
  4. ping [remote IP, e.g., 8.8.8.8] — tests routing and internet reachability.
  5. ping [hostname] — tests DNS + all of the above.

When ping fails but connectivity works: Some devices and firewalls block ICMP for security reasons. A host may be fully reachable via TCP (HTTP, SSH) but drop ICMP. Don't assume 'ping fails = device down' — try other protocols too.

hack.memory: ping = 'are you there?' ICMP Echo Request/Reply. Success symbols: !=yes, .=timeout, U=unreachable. Windows stops at 4. Linux/Mac goes forever (-c to limit). Cisco stops at 5. Mnemonic: 'ping pong' — you throw the ball (request), the other side throws it back (reply). No return = ping fails.
hack.practice: Run ping to 4 targets: (1) 127.0.0.1, (2) your own IP, (3) your gateway, (4) 8.8.8.8. All 4 should succeed. Note RTTs — loopback ~0ms, gateway 1-5ms, 8.8.8.8 typically 10-50ms depending on your ISP. Then ping google.com — watch DNS resolve the name first, then ICMP begin. On Cisco router, type ping in exec mode and interpret !!!!! vs ......
hack.meta: Most fundamental network tool — on every exam, in every job. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and every lab video after. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Know the Cisco symbols cold (! . U). Know the ladder steps. Practice reading ping output until it's automatic.

1.10.e.info:
traceroute (Linux/macOS) / tracert (Windows) maps the hop-by-hop path a packet takes from source to destination. Each hop is a router along the way, identified by its IP address and round-trip time. When ping succeeds but performance is terrible, or ping fails and you want to know WHERE it's failing, traceroute tells you exactly which router in the path is the problem.

How it works — the TTL trick:

  1. Traceroute sends a packet with TTL = 1. The first router decrements TTL to 0 and drops the packet, sending back an ICMP Time Exceeded message. Traceroute records the router's IP.
  2. Next packet has TTL = 2. Gets past router 1, router 2 decrements to 0 and sends Time Exceeded. Recorded.
  3. Continues until TTL equals the distance to the destination, or until max hops (default 30).
  4. The last hop (the actual destination) responds with ICMP Echo Reply (if Windows tracert uses ICMP) or ICMP Port Unreachable (if Linux/Mac traceroute uses UDP to a high port that's not listening). Either way, that response ends the trace.

Probe protocols by OS:

  • Windows tracert — ICMP Echo Request (same as ping).
  • Linux/macOS traceroute — UDP to high ports (33434+) by default. Use -I for ICMP, -T for TCP SYN (tcp traceroute is useful for firewall-heavy paths).

Reading the output:

Tracing route to google.com [142.250.80.46]:
  1    1 ms    1 ms    1 ms  192.168.1.1
  2    8 ms    7 ms    9 ms  10.10.10.1
  3   12 ms   11 ms   13 ms  74.125.242.1
  4    *       *       *     Request timed out.
  5   18 ms   19 ms   18 ms  142.250.80.46
Trace complete.
  • Each row = 1 hop. Columns = 3 RTT measurements.
  • * * * = no response from that hop. Could be a router that filters ICMP/UDP probes, OR packet loss at that hop. Doesn't necessarily mean traffic is being dropped — the router might just refuse to reply.
  • Last line shows the destination. If you reach the final IP, the trace succeeded.

Using traceroute for troubleshooting:

  • If trace completes and RTT is reasonable → path is fine.
  • If trace stops at hop N (all subsequent hops are * * *) → problem is between hop N and hop N+1. Call that network segment's admin.
  • If RTT spikes dramatically at a specific hop → congestion or geographic distance at that point.
  • If the path takes a weird detour (e.g., suddenly routes through another country) → BGP issue or suboptimal routing.

hack.memory: Traceroute = 'show me the path.' TTL trick: send 1, 2, 3... each is dropped at the corresponding hop, which returns Time Exceeded. Windows = tracert (ICMP). Linux/Mac = traceroute (UDP). * * * = hop not responding (filtered, not necessarily broken). Last responding hop = your suspect.
hack.practice: Run tracert google.com (Windows) or traceroute google.com (Mac/Linux). Count the hops to google. Identify your ISP's routers by their hostnames. Look for any * * * — usually firewalls or filtered routers. Now trace a weird target like a server on another continent and watch the RTTs grow with geographic distance.
hack.meta: Common exam question. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Know: Windows tracert uses ICMP, Linux traceroute uses UDP by default. Know TTL mechanics (each router decrements, 0 = Time Exceeded). Know * * * means hop not responding. This comes up in design questions and troubleshooting scenarios.

1.10.e.info:
nslookup is the cross-platform DNS query tool. It asks a DNS server to resolve a hostname to an IP (or an IP to a hostname, a reverse lookup). Available on Windows, Linux, and macOS — making it the go-to 'does DNS work?' test regardless of OS.

Interactive vs command-line mode:

  • Command-line: nslookup google.com — one-shot query, returns result, exits.
  • Interactive: nslookup (no args) — opens a prompt where you can type multiple queries, change servers (server 8.8.8.8), change query types (set type=mx), etc.

Typical output:

Server:   192.168.1.1
Address:  192.168.1.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:     google.com
Address:  142.250.80.46
  • Server — which DNS server the host is querying (from OS config).
  • Address — the DNS server IP and port (53 = DNS).
  • Non-authoritative answer — this DNS server is a resolver/cache, not the authoritative owner of the domain. Typical for ISP or public DNS.
  • Name + Address — the resolved hostname and its IP.

Querying different record types:

  • nslookup google.com → A record (IPv4).
  • nslookup -type=aaaa google.com → AAAA record (IPv6).
  • nslookup -type=mx google.com → MX records (mail servers).
  • nslookup -type=ns google.com → NS records (authoritative name servers).
  • nslookup -type=txt _dmarc.google.com → TXT records (DMARC, SPF).
  • nslookup 8.8.8.8 → reverse lookup (IP to PTR record).

Querying a specific DNS server:

nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8

This queries 8.8.8.8 instead of the OS-configured DNS. Useful for testing whether your configured DNS is broken vs the internet's DNS in general.

Common diagnostic use:

  1. Standard nslookup google.com fails → DNS is broken.
  2. nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8 succeeds → your configured DNS is the problem (not routing, not internet).
  3. nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8 also fails → routing to 8.8.8.8 is broken (not DNS specifically).

Windows vs Unix: nslookup works similarly on both. On Linux/macOS, dig is considered more powerful and modern, but nslookup is still universally available and adequate for most checks.

hack.memory: nslookup = 'name server lookup.' Universal DNS query tool. nslookup google.com = 'resolve this name.' nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8 = 'resolve it using this specific DNS server.' Reverse: nslookup 8.8.8.8 returns the PTR. Mnemonic: 'NSL = Name Server Lookup.'
hack.practice: Run nslookup google.com on any OS. Note the server (your configured DNS) and the returned IP. Then nslookup google.com 1.1.1.1 — compare to Cloudflare's answer. Try nslookup -type=mx gmail.com — see Google's mail servers. Reverse: nslookup 142.250.80.46 — returns a Google DNS hostname. Each of these is an exam-relevant scenario.
hack.meta: High exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 and Day 48 (DNS). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 and Chapter 18. Know nslookup works on Windows, Linux, and macOS. Know how to specify a DNS server as the second argument. Know it queries the configured DNS by default.

1.10.e.info:
dig (Domain Information Groper) is the modern, detailed DNS lookup tool on Linux and macOS. Windows does NOT have dig by default (though you can install it from BIND tools). Where nslookup gives a summary, dig gives the full DNS response packet breakdown — query section, answer section, authority section, additional section, and query time.

Basic usage:

dig google.com

Sample output (trimmed):

; <<>> DiG 9.10.6 <<>> google.com
;; global options: +cmd
;; Got answer:
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 12345
;; flags: qr rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; QUESTION SECTION:
;google.com.        IN  A

;; ANSWER SECTION:
google.com.    300  IN  A   142.250.80.46

;; Query time: 14 msec
;; SERVER: 192.168.1.1#53(192.168.1.1)
;; WHEN: Wed Apr 16 10:00:00 CDT 2026
;; MSG SIZE  rcvd: 55

Reading dig output:

  • HEADER — status (NOERROR = good, NXDOMAIN = name doesn't exist, SERVFAIL = server error).
  • flags — qr=query response, rd=recursion desired, ra=recursion available. aa=authoritative answer (you queried the authoritative server directly, not a cache).
  • QUESTION SECTION — what was asked (name, class IN=internet, type A).
  • ANSWER SECTION — the resolved records. The number (300 here) is the TTL in seconds.
  • Query time — how long the resolver took. Low = cached, high = fresh recursive lookup.
  • SERVER — which DNS server was queried.

Common dig flags:

  • dig google.com MX — query MX records (or A, AAAA, NS, TXT, CNAME, SOA, PTR).
  • dig google.com +short — just the answer, no header/stats.
  • dig google.com @8.8.8.8 — query a specific DNS server.
  • dig -x 8.8.8.8 — reverse lookup (IP to PTR).
  • dig google.com +trace — show full delegation from root to authoritative — great for understanding DNS hierarchy.

dig vs nslookup:

  • dig — full packet detail, modern, more flags, Linux/macOS default.
  • nslookup — simpler summary, universal (including Windows), interactive mode.

Exam relevance: Medium. Know that dig is the preferred DNS tool on Linux/macOS. Know it's not on Windows by default. Know the basic output (answer section, TTL).

hack.memory: dig = 'DNS Information Groper' — Linux/Mac only, more powerful than nslookup. dig google.com +short = quick answer. dig google.com @8.8.8.8 = ask a specific server. dig -x [IP] = reverse lookup. Mnemonic: 'dig deeper than nslookup.'
hack.practice: On Linux/Mac: dig google.com — read all sections. dig google.com +short — just the answer. dig google.com MX @1.1.1.1 — ask Cloudflare for MX records. dig -x 8.8.8.8 — reverse. dig google.com +trace — watch the resolver walk the root → .com → google.com hierarchy. Each command teaches a different DNS concept.
hack.meta: Medium exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 mentions dig. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Know: dig is Linux/Mac, nslookup is universal. Know +short and @server syntax. The exam won't require deep dig mastery but will expect you to recognize it as the Unix DNS tool.

1.10.e.info:
pathping is a Windows-only command that combines ping and tracert into a single, more thorough path analysis. First it traces the hops to the destination (like tracert). Then it pauses and sends 100 pings to each hop to calculate precise packet loss and latency statistics. This reveals intermittent problems that a single traceroute or ping would miss.

How pathping works:

  1. Phase 1 — Trace: Discovers the hops to the destination using TTL manipulation. Prints the route.
  2. Phase 2 — Wait: Shows 'Computing statistics for 25 seconds...' (or longer depending on hop count).
  3. Phase 3 — Stats: Sends 100 pings to each hop, reports lost/sent per hop AND end-to-end loss between consecutive hops.

Example output (Phase 3):

Hop  RTT    Lost/Sent = Pct   Address
  0                                 MYPC [192.168.1.50]
                0/ 100 =  0%  |
  1    1ms   0/ 100 =  0%     0/ 100 =  0%    192.168.1.1
                1/ 100 =  1%  |
  2    8ms   1/ 100 =  1%     0/ 100 =  0%    10.10.10.1
                0/ 100 =  0%  |
  3   12ms   1/ 100 =  1%     0/ 100 =  0%    74.125.242.1

Reading the output:

  • RTT column — average round-trip time to that hop.
  • Lost/Sent = Pct (first column for this hop) — cumulative loss from source to this hop.
  • Lost/Sent = Pct (between hops, next to the vertical bar) — loss BETWEEN the previous hop and this hop. THIS is the key metric — it tells you which single link is dropping packets.

When to use pathping:

  • Users report 'intermittent' network problems that regular ping/tracert don't reveal.
  • You suspect packet loss somewhere in the path and need to pinpoint which hop.
  • You want statistical evidence to send to a network provider or team.

Downsides: Pathping takes several MINUTES to complete because it needs time to gather statistics. It's also Windows-only — Linux/Mac admins use tools like mtr for similar functionality.

Comparable Linux/Mac tool: mtr (My Traceroute) is the cross-platform equivalent. It continuously traces and pings in real-time with a live-updating display. Often more useful than pathping because it shows continuous data.

hack.memory: pathping = tracert + 100 pings per hop = 'precision path analysis.' Windows-only. Takes several minutes. Between-hop loss is the key column — tells you which link is dropping packets. Linux equivalent: mtr. Mnemonic: 'path + ping = pathping.'
hack.practice: On Windows: pathping google.com. Watch the phases — trace, pause, stats. Read the output. Compare to a single tracert google.com — pathping has much more statistical detail. On Linux/Mac: install mtr (sudo apt install mtr or brew install mtr) and run mtr google.com for the live-updating equivalent.
hack.meta: Low exam frequency. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 briefly covers pathping. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Likely 1 question at most — 'which Windows command combines ping and tracert?' → pathping. Or 'what tool gives per-hop packet loss stats on Windows?' → pathping.

1.10.e.info:
The troubleshooting ladder is a systematic, bottom-up sequence for isolating IP connectivity problems. Run the steps in order — each one proves a layer of the stack is working. The step that fails tells you exactly where the problem is. This discipline turns random 'try stuff' troubleshooting into a repeatable 2-minute diagnosis.

The 5 steps:

  1. ping 127.0.0.1 — local stack. Tests the OS TCP/IP implementation. Should ALWAYS succeed. If it fails, reinstall networking components or reboot. Extremely rare failure.
  2. ping [own IP] — NIC. Tests the network adapter (hardware/driver). Should succeed if the NIC is functional. If it fails, driver issue, hardware failure, or interface is administratively down.
  3. ping [default gateway] — local network. Tests L1/L2/L3 connectivity to the router. If it fails, check: cable, switch port, VLAN, IP/mask, gateway IP correctness, ARP (can you ARP for the gateway?).
  4. ping [known remote IP, e.g., 8.8.8.8] — routing + internet. Tests whether traffic can leave your subnet and reach the outside world. If step 3 worked but this fails: router's internet link is down, ISP issue, ACL blocking, or the target is actually down.
  5. ping [hostname, e.g., google.com] — DNS + everything above. Tests DNS resolution on top of all the above layers. If step 4 worked but this fails: DNS is broken (server unreachable, wrong server, expired cache).

Why bottom-up works: By testing the most local thing first and expanding outward, you don't chase phantom problems. Network issues cascade — if the gateway is unreachable, everything else WILL fail. There's no point running nslookup if you can't even reach your gateway. Fix the lowest layer first.

Failure-to-diagnosis map:

  • Step 1 fails → OS networking is broken. Reinstall stack, reboot, scan for corruption.
  • Step 2 fails → NIC broken or interface down. Check ipconfig, enable the adapter, driver.
  • Step 3 fails → local network issue. Cable? VLAN? Wrong mask? Wrong gateway? ARP?
  • Step 4 fails → routing/ISP issue. Check gateway's upstream, ISP status, ACLs.
  • Step 5 fails → DNS issue. Flush cache, check configured DNS, try 8.8.8.8.

Real-world time savings: A user reports 'internet is down.' Walk through the ladder: steps 1-4 all succeed, step 5 fails. 30 seconds to diagnosis: 'your DNS is broken.' Fix: ipconfig /flushdns and/or change DNS to 8.8.8.8. Done in 60 seconds total. The alternative — random troubleshooting — can take 30+ minutes.

Exam relevance: Very high. The exam frequently presents scenarios like 'user cannot reach www.example.com but can ping 10.1.1.1' and expects you to instantly identify the broken step and the responsible parameter. Memorize the ladder and the failure mappings.

hack.memory: Mnemonic: 'Lonely Iguanas Go Running Daily' = Loopback, Interface, Gateway, Remote, DNS. Each step tests one layer higher. Step that fails = where the break is. Step 5 fails alone = DNS. Step 4 fails alone = routing. Step 3 fails alone = local network. Step 2 fails alone = NIC. Step 1 fails = OS stack (very rare).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build a basic network with PC → switch → router → remote server. From the PC, execute the 5-step ladder. All should succeed. Then introduce faults one at a time (break step by step): (a) set wrong own IP, (b) remove gateway, (c) disconnect router's uplink, (d) set wrong DNS. Re-run the ladder each time and see exactly which step breaks. Five break-and-fix drills = permanent muscle memory.
hack.meta: THE single highest-yield troubleshooting skill on the CCNA. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 5 demonstrates this exactly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. The exam is full of 'user can X but can't Y' questions where the ladder lets you answer in seconds. Master this and you'll save 3-5 minutes per troubleshooting question.
